summary.intent: Optimize the `saveGitOidMapping` call in the indexer to avoid redundant DB reads on every index pass for every checkpoint.
summary.outcome: Pre-fetching was implemented as a `Map<checkpointId, gitOid>` so that the mapping logic can skip already-matched pairs and handle rebase scenarios where the same checkpoint ID maps to a new git OID.

repo: The `gitOid` field is `@id` in the Prisma schema, meaning the mapping table is keyed by git OID, not checkpoint ID â€” upsert logic must account for this.
The indexer has an `indexAllCheckpointsV2` orchestrator that calls `indexCheckpointV2` per checkpoint; cross-checkpoint state can be threaded via parameters.
After a rebase, a checkpoint ID can legitimately be remapped to a new git OID, so presence-only caching is insufficient â€” the full (checkpointId, gitOid) pair must be validated.

code.finding: `indexAllCheckpointsV2` pre-fetches all existing checkpointâ†’gitOid mappings into a `Map<string, string>` before the loop; this map is passed to `indexCheckpointV2` as `mappedCheckpointIds`.

`saveGitOidMapping` now owns the skip/upsert logic: it resolves the commit OID first, checks the map for an exact (checkpointId, gitOid) pair match, skips if identical, and upserts if the OID changed.

workflow: Running `npm run build` after edits was used as a fast compile-time correctness check before considering the task done.
Requirement clarification mid-implementation (rebase scenario) prompted a design pivot from `Set` to `Map` â€” getting the data model right before coding avoids rework.

friction: Initial design used a `Set<checkpointId>` which was invalidated by the rebase use-case, requiring a second round of edits to switch to `Map<checkpointId, gitOid>`.
open_items: After a successful upsert (OID changed), the in-memory map should be updated so subsequent checkpoints in the same pass reflect the new mapping â€” verify this update is present in the final implementation.

The `saveGitOidMapping` function now resolves the git commit OID on every call even for skipped checkpoints; consider whether the OID resolution itself is expensive and should also be short-circuited.
